source_title: How to Get Rich: Every Episode

paragraphs.speaker: nivi
paragraphs.name: Nivi
paragraphs.text: This is one giant megasode that collects every episode we've done on getting rich. All of it based on Naval's tweetstorm of how to get rich without getting lucky. We're going to be talking about his epic tweetstorm on how to get rich without getting lucky.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Wealth is assets that earn while you sleep. Wealth is the factory, the robots cranking out things. Wealth is the computer program that's running at night, serving other customers. Even money in the bank that is being reinvested into other assets and businesses. The reason you want wealth is because it buys you freedom. So you don't have to wear a tie like a collar around your neck. So you don't have to wake up at 7 a.m. and rush to work. The purpose of wealth is freedom. It's nothing more than that.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Money is how we transfer wealth. Money is social credits. It is the ability to have credits and debits on other people's time. If I create value for society, society says, oh, thank you. We owe you something in the future for the work that you did in the past. Here's a little IOU. Let's call that money.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Status is just your ranking in the social hierarchy. Wealth is not a zero sum game. Everybody in the world can have a house. If anything, the more houses that are built, the easier it becomes to build houses. We create things together. Status on the other hand is a zero sum game. For number three to move to number two, number two has to move out of that slot. Politics is an example of a status game. Sports is an example. To be the winner, there must be a loser.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Making money is not evil. This idea that it's stolen is this horrible zero sum game that people who are trying to gain status play. But the reality is everyone can be rich. In the first world, everyone is richer than almost anyone who was alive 200 years ago. 200 years ago, nobody had antibiotics, nobody had cars, nobody had electricity, nobody had the iPhone. Today I would rather be a poor person in the first world country than be a rich person in Louis the 14th France.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: Capitalism is intrinsic to the human species. Capitalism is not something we invented. It is innate to us in every exchange that we have. When you and I exchange information, I want some information back from you. The notion of exchange and keeping track of credits and debits, this is built into us as flexible social animals. We are the only animals in the animal kingdom that cooperate across genetic boundaries.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: There are fundamentally four kinds of luck. The first kind is just blind luck where something completely out of my control happened. The second kind comes through persistence, hard work, hustle, motion. You're just running around creating lots of opportunities, generating a lot of energy. A third way is that you become very good at spotting luck. If you are very skilled in a field, you will notice when a lucky break happens. And then the last kind is the weirdest, hardest kind, which is where you build a unique character, a unique brand, a unique mindset where then luck finds you.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: You're not going to get rich renting out your time. You must own equity, a piece of a business to gain your financial freedom. Your inputs are very closely tied to your outputs. When you're sleeping, you're not earning. When you're retired, you're not earning. You're replaceable because you're doing a set role. Most set roles can be taught. If they can be taught in a school, then eventually you'll be competing with someone who's got more recent knowledge.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: You want to look for professions and careers where the inputs and the outputs are highly disconnected. Tools and leverage are what create this disconnection between inputs and outputs. The higher the creativity component of a profession, the more likely it is to have disconnected inputs and outputs. One great engineer can create Bitcoin and create billions of dollars worth of value. An engineer working on the wrong thing can work for an entire year, and every piece of code they ship ends up not getting used.

paragraphs.speaker: naval
paragraphs.name: Naval Ravikant
paragraphs.text: The Internet has massively broadened the possible space of careers. The fundamental property of the Internet is it connects every human to each other human on the planet. You can now reach everyone. What the Internet does is allows any niche obsession to scale. Whatever niche obsession you have, the Internet allows you to scale.
